preamble: Shift of bilabial fricative [ɸ] to labiodental fricative [f], driven by the slight articulatory advantage of anchoring the lower lip against the upper teeth rather than opposing the two lips. This is one of the most cross-linguistically common fricative changes and has occurred independently in multiple unrelated families.

examples.note: The letter phi, originally an aspirated stop [pʰ] in Classical Greek, had shifted to bilabial [ɸ] by the Hellenistic period and then merged with labiodental [f] in Byzantine Greek, the value it retains in Modern Greek.

While Standard Ewe preserves a bilabial–labiodental contrast (/ɸ/ vs. /f/), several dialects and related Kwa languages have neutralized it in favor of labiodental [f], illustrating the typological pressure behind this shift.
Proto-Athabaskan labial fricatives shifted to labiodental articulation in several daughter languages, with Chipewyan retaining evidence of the transitional stage.

sources: Horrocks, G. (2010). Greek: A History of the Language and Its Speakers. Wiley-Blackwell.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
Clements, G. N., & Rialland, A. (2008). Africa as a phonological area. In B. Heine & D. Nurse (eds.), A Linguistic Geography of Africa.
